data.title: CES Energy Solutions: Still Attractive Despite Strong YTD Share Price Performance
data.description:
Introduction

CES Energy Solutions Corp. (TSX:CEU:CA, OTCPK:CESDF) is a Canadian company focusing on the development and production of consumable fluids and specialty chemicals for the North American oil and gas industry. The company is active throughout the USA and Canada (with 71% of the Q2 revenue generated in the USA), and has exposure to several segments of the oil and gas industry. The image below shows where CES is active, and which trade names it has in its portfolio.

The company serves a wide range of clients, with approximately 55% of its total revenue coming from customers with a market cap of in excess of $10B, ranging up to market caps of $700B.

The company currently has 219M shares outstanding, resulting in a market capitalization of C$1.9B, based on the current share price of approximately C$8.8.

A strong earnings and cash flow profile

The company’s financial results are subject to seasonal changes as the second quarter of the year represents the spring break-up period, resulting in the lowest level of activities throughout the year. The winter drill period tends to be the most active, while the summer months have ‘medium’ activity levels. This means we shouldn’t extrapolate quarterly results to derive the full-year expectations and although I’ll discuss the recently published Q2 results, I will subsequently take a step back and look at the H1 result in general.

In the second quarter of the year, CES reported a total revenue of almost C$574M, which allowed it to report an operating profit of just under C$59M. As the company also recorded a net finance income, its pre-tax profit was boosted to C$64M, on which just over C$12M in taxes was owed. This resulted in a net profit of C$51.8M for an EPS of C$0.23.

As you can see in the image above, the net finance income was a nice tailwind, as the company had reported a net finance expense of approximately C$17.5M in the first quarter of the year. As you can see below, that (likely non-recurring) finance income item was related to a C$17.4M FX gain, partially offset by a C$3.4M derivative loss.

As the image above also shows, the "pure" interest expenses are approximately C$8.5M per quarter and thus around C$17M per semester. This indeed means the recorded net finance cost of C$12.4M in the entire first semester is a better (but still not perfect) representation of the underlying situation.

That’s also why I like to keep an eye on a company’s cash flow statement as well. As you can see below, CES reported an operating cash flow of C$76.7M before taking changes in the working capital position into account. But from that number we still have to deduct C$11.3M in lease payments. This means the underlying operating cash flow was C$65.4M.

The total capex incurred during the second quarter was approximately C$25.2M (excluding the C$7M cash outflow related to an acquisition), indicating a net free cash flow of C$40M. Divided over the 219M shares outstanding (and this number is decreasing), CES Energy Solutions reported a FCFPS of C$0.23 in the second quarter of the year, and approximately C$0.34 per share in the entire first half of the year.

That sounds a bit light, but keep in mind the timing of the capital expenditures plays a role here. CES spent in excess of C$58M on capex items and almost C$21M on lease payments for a total of C$79M versus the total depreciation and amortization expenses of just over C$50M.

It is important to realize CES has been guiding for a full-year capex of C$80M (which means almost 75% has already been spent in the first semester), with only half of that (C$40M) representing maintenance capex. This means the normalized maintenance capex incurred in the first half of the year would be C$20M on a pro forma basis.

And that’s important to put the weak FCFPS into perspective. On average, only C$20M of the C$58M in incurred capex would be deemed maintenance capex. And using that number, the underlying sustaining free cash flow result would be approximately C$113M or C$0.51 per share. On an annual basis, we are looking at a FCF yield of approximately 13-14%.

The balance sheet remains robust - allowing for share repurchases

The very strong underlying cash flow result allows CES to invest in growth (given the anticipated C$40M in growth capex this year) while aggressively buying back its own shares.

That’s enabled by the robust balance sheet. At the end of the second quarter, CES Energy Solutions had a positive working capital position of in excess of C$600M while its net debt (excluding lease liabilities) was just C$374M. C$200M of this amount comes from the 2029 senior notes while C$168M was drawn from the senior credit facility (with an additional C$382M available to draw down). And as you can see below, the company comfortably meets all covenants.

CES Energy Solutions currently pays a quarterly dividend of just C$0.0425 per share for C$0.17 per share per year, representing a dividend yield of less than 2%. A large portion of its underlying free cash flow is spent on share buybacks, having spent C$31.3M in Q2 and C$52.6M in the entire first half of the year. This allowed the company to reduce its net share count by approximately 5.4M shares, and CES may very well end the year with a net share count of just around 215M shares. It goes without saying this will further enhance the per-share metrics.

Investment thesis

CES Energy Solutions has an enviable position in the drilling fluids market, and is currently participating in some larger tender offers (and it expects to win a few of those). About 75% of the tender offers it is currently participating in are from potential new clients which will allow CES to gain some market share. The company has been hiring additional employees that will allow them to hit the ground running upon winning a/multiple tenders which also means that if CES would not win any new contracts, the G&A expenses will likely decrease again as the headcount will be right-sized again.

I currently have no position in CES Energy Solutions, but I am definitely keeping an eye on the stock now. The oil services segment is cheap in general, but I like having exposure to the chemicals too, rather than just owning drilling & completion services.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Valuation Vs. Reality: Why Firefly Aerospace's Stock Faces Significant Downside
data.description:
Space and defense technology company Firefly Aerospace Inc. (NASDAQ:FLY) stands at a critical juncture. While it possesses undeniable strengths that have positioned it as a significant player in the rapidly growing global space economy, a rigorous financial analysis reveals severe underlying challenges. Despite its rapid revenue growth, Firefly is plagued by deep and worsening losses, a very high customer concentration, and a cash burn rate that threatens its financial stability. The company’s valuation implies growth expectations that are historically improbable and fundamentally unsustainable. Consequently, I assign a “Sell” rating to the company, as the great optimism embedded in its valuation fails to account for the significant execution risks and competitive pressures it faces.

Firefly’s Strategic Challenges in the Global Space Economy

Firefly provides responsive and reliable mission solutions. Its key offerings are the Alpha launch vehicle, which has a proven flight record, and the larger, in-development Eclipse rocket. Its success, including a moon landing, makes it a “partner of choice for national security, government, and commercial customers”.

The growing space economy, which encompasses “launch, spacecraft, satellite constellations, and on-orbit and in-space services”, has become increasingly attractive, with competition breaking down along the following lines: providers of dedicated and rideshare launch vehicles; providers of spacecraft and satellite constellations, and providers of launch and spacecraft solutions for on-orbit and in-space services. Management cites a 2024 McKinsey & Company report which says that,

We estimate that the global space economy will be worth $1.8 trillion by 2035 (accounting for inflation), up from $630 billion in 2023. This figure includes both “backbone” applications—such as those for satellites, launchers, and services like broadcast television or GPS—and what we term “reach” applications”—those for which space technology helps companies across industries generate revenues. Uber, for example, relies on the combination of satellite signals and chips inside smartphones to connect drivers and riders and provide directions in every city.

In 2023, backbone applications made up $330 billion, or slightly greater than 50 percent, of the global space economy, while reach applications represented $300 billion. The expected annual growth rate for backbone and reach applications is twice the projected rate of GDP growth over the next decade (exhibit). For comparison, the value estimates for space are similar to those for semiconductors (estimated at $600 billion in 2021 with 6 to 8 percent annual growth into the 2030s) and roughly half of the projection for the global payments industry (estimated to reach $3.2 trillion in revenues by 2027).

Firefly’s growth in the global space economy seems to be a consequence of its being one of the few US commercial firms who can reliably provide access to launch, transit and operations in space. Even without the reshoring that has begun, given the national security importance of the global space economy, the company is likely to see some growth.

However, it is almost a law of capital that the prospect of attractive returns draws in new capital, and this is true of the global space economy. In fulfilling its mission, the company faces enormous competition in the global space industry, competition that has only grown with time. As the company explained in its S-1/A,

Competitors across our launch, land, and orbit businesses range in size from divisions of large public corporations to small, privately held entities. We face intense competition in the global space market. In addition, we are aware of a significant number of competitors actively engaged in developing commercial launch capabilities for small and medium sized payloads in the U.S. and globally. Many of our current and potential competitors are larger and have substantially greater financial or other resources than we currently have or expect to have in the future, and thus may be better positioned to exploit the market need for spacecraft propulsion systems and launch services for small and medium sized payloads with targeted orbital delivery, which is one primary focus of our launch business.

Even foreign competition could hurt the company domestically, by constraining the price that the company could set for its services. At present, the company has significant competitive advantages with its unique "one-stop shop" model offering launch vehicles, landers, and orbital spacecraft; its patented technology, a record of rapid-response missions for national security; and a robust $1.1 billion backlog backed by strong partnerships with major agencies and defense contractors. In addition, the company’s vertically integrated, purpose-built infrastructure is a formidable barrier to entry. Nevertheless, as the industry consolidates, and new capital enters these competitive advantages are under threat. With increased competition will come an eroding of NOPAT margins, and a decline in return on invested capital (ROIC).

Customer Concentration is High

In its S-1/A, management noted that for “the three months ended March 31, 2025, our top five customers together accounted for over 99% of our revenue and our top five backlog customers accounted for approximately 92% of our backlog as of March 31, 2025.” The risks are obvious: any diminishment in volumes from these customers will have severe consequences for the firm’s overall revenues. In addition, because the firm is so dependent on so few customers, it is in a weak negotiating position when it comes to pricing negotiations, making it improbable that the firm can achieve the kind of net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) margin expansion that it requires to justify the current price.

In addition, in an era in which the federal government is scaling back its commitments, it is notable that,

A substantial amount of our backlog for government customers is also subject to risks of future government funding levels, which may be substantially curtailed or abandoned, resulting in contract cancellations, modifications, delays, or reduction in orders. In particular, the current administration has indicated it is committed to decreasing federal spending and the size of government. If the administration were to take actions that impacted the amount our government customers are able to spend on our services, it could materially adversely impact our business, results of operations, and financial condition.

Lacking the kind of customer diversification needed for a robust revenue stream, Firefly’s business is potentially fragile. Indeed, with both Republicans and Democrats seeking to reduce spending, management emphasised repeatedly in its prospectus that future federal government funding cuts "could directly affect our financial performance and could have a material adverse effect on our business, results of operations, prospects, and financial condition.”

Rapid Revenue Growth Amid Persistent Profitability Challenges

Academic research finds that IPO firms, pre-IPO, typically enjoy high levels of growth, and that post-IPO, their growth tends to decline. While one cannot say if decline will be true of Firefly, what one can say is that, since 2023, it has compounded its revenue by a remarkable 36.42% a year, from $55.24 million in 2023 to $102.81 million in the last twelve months (LTM). In the company’s Q2 2025 earnings release, management guided for full-year 2025 revenue of between $133 and $145 million, indicating that revenue at least will continue to grow for the near-term.

The firm’s NOPAT, however, has steadily declined, from -$131.88 million to -$221.69 million in that time. The company’s struggles with profitability are also fairly atypical: academic research shows that 85% of US-based IPOs in 2024 were unprofitable. While NOPAT has declined, the firm’s NOPAT margin improved from -238.75% in 2023 to -215.64% in the LTM. The company’s balance sheet efficiency similarly declined in that time, with invested capital turns dipping from 0.13 to 0.11.

As a growing, unprofitable company investing heavily in expanding the business, the company’s free cash flows (FCF) are obviously negative, declining from -$501.11 million in 2023 to -$386.66 million in the LTM. The company’s LTM FCF has an unattractive yield of -5.3%. The company’s cash burn is significant, with Firefly burning the equivalent of 18.84% of its current market cap, since 2023. (In terms of GAAP FCF that cash burn is much lower, at $545.18 million, or 8.99% of current market cap).

Since Alfred Marshall first published the Principles of Economics in 1890, economists have known that a firm creates value when it earns a ROIC in excess of its cost of capital. Firefly has not been able to pass this “one dollar test”. In other words, a dollar invested in the company would have been better off in the market. While ROIC has risen from -30.89% in 2023 to -22.88% in the LTM, in the LTM, the company has earned -$305.90 million in economic profits.

Market Price Implies Extreme Growth Expectations

At the current price, Firefly has an economic book value (EBV) of -$3.5 billion. EBV is the company’s value today if it didn’t create any value from new investments. In other words, Firefly's entire market capitalization is based on expectations for future value creation. It is important to remember that a company’s value is made up of two things: what it is worth before management undertakes any value-creating activities, its EBV, and the present value of growth opportunities (PVGO), the extra value the market expects from future growth.

Knowing that a share price carries with it assumptions about the performance of operating value drivers, revenue growth, NOPAT margins, and the incremental investment rate, I used my reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to estimate these price-implied expectations and stress-test them. In all scenarios, I assume that the Firefly maintains its current incremental investment rate. The LTM incremental working capital investment rate is fairly high at 151.36%, and the reader might wonder if it will not fall. I freeze the rate in order to focus on revenue and NOPAT margin. While there is no quantitative guidance on future investment rates, management remarks in the prospectus that,

The growth of our business is dependent on our ability to improve our profit margins over time while successfully scaling our business, including through continued investment in initiatives to improve our operating leverage.

Later, it adds that,

To continue gaining market share and attracting customers, we plan to continue to make substantial investments in R&D for the continued enhancements of our Launch and Spacecraft Solutions. Our growth opportunity is dependent on our continued ability to expand our addressable launch market, win lunar and orbital missions and expand our portfolio of services related to those offerings.

Without any signals of a material change in direction in terms of investment, the simplest modelling decision is to hold the investment rate constant. Indeed, as investment rates are often driven by wider industry effects, revenue and NOPAT margin more clearly reflect managerial decisions. A much lower investment rate would of course ease the company’s path toward positive FCF and help the company in achieving sustainable profitability. Nevertheless, the assumption of a persistently high investment rate reflects management’s own guidance and aligns with the logic of frontier-market business models, where growth requires heavy reinvestment. The reader may view the accompanying spreadsheet to understand the following logic.

Firstly, if

  • revenue rises to $145 million in 2025, the upper bound of management’s guidance, and compounds by 62.02% a year, thereafter, the firm’s 2-year CAGR at that point, and,

  • NOPAT margin surges immediately to 15%, then,

Firefly has a market-implied competitive advantage period (MICAP) of 12 years, i.e. the market expects Firefly to create value until 2037, at which point, its shareholder value per share equals its current stock price, and the firm posts $47.45 billion in revenue and earns $7.12 billion in NOPAT. In the accompanying spreadsheet, the MICAP year, 2037 is highlighted.

(As the spreadsheet is live, the MICAP is constantly being calculated, so the reader should check at the bottom of the sheet "Price-Implied Expectations ((PIE))", for what the current MICAP is. I have left the periods after that open so that if the MICAP changes as a consequence of a meaningful share price increase, the reader can see what the new MICAP is using the current inputs. This is done only so that the spreadsheet has a long shelf life. It is not meant to indicate that revenue growth and NOPAT margins continue as given. Strictly, the reader should think of the post 2037 period as one in which the company's ROIC equals or is below the cost of capital, i.e. when the firm no longer creates value.)

If, however, these lofty expectations are not met, and,

  • Revenue rises to $133 million in 2025, the lower bound of management’s guidance, and compounds by 55.17% a year thereafter, the firm’s 2-year CAGR at that point, and,

  • NOPAT margin is 15%, then,

Firefly posts $25.9 billion in revenue and earns $3.89 billion in NOPAT in 2037, and the share is worth $17.96 today, a downside of 63.73% from the current price.

Finally, if,

  • Revenue rises to $133 million in 2025, and compounds by 40% a year thereafter, and,

  • NOPAT margin is 15%, then,

Firefly posts $7.54 billion in revenue and earns $1.13 billion in NOPAT in 2037, and the share is worth $1.98 today, a downside of 96% from the current price.

It should be noted that it is highly unlikely that any firm can sustain 40%-plus revenue growth for a decade. According to Credit Suisse’s “The Base Rate Book”, between 1950 and 2015, just 0.5% of firms enjoyed a 10-year sales CAGR of more than 40%. Moreover, in the firm’s prospectus, management warned that,

We expect to continue to incur net losses for the next several years and we may not achieve or maintain profitability in the future.

…We cannot assure you that we will ever achieve or sustain profitability and may continue to incur significant losses going forward. Any failure by us to achieve or sustain profitability on a consistent basis could cause the value of our common stock to decline.

Sell

Although Firefly operates in an attractive market and has shown impressive technical capabilities, capabilities that have spurred its growth, its present valuation is not justifiable. Firefly’s potential fragilities due to heavy customer concentration, increasing competition, and an inability to achieve profitability. Looming large over the company’s prospects is the potential for federal government budget cuts that could impede the company’s growth. As a consequence, there is a large gap between what the company’s operating history suggests it can achieve and what the market expects of it. This results in severe downsides. As a result, I assign a “Sell” rating to the firm.

data.title: Amgen: Imdelltra Could Be The Next Game Changer
data.description:
It's been just over three months since my last article on Amgen Inc. (NASDAQ:AMGN), "Amgen: Imdelltra Data and Q1 Results Impressive."

In it, I mainly presented an analysis of clinical data on its "nascent blockbuster" called Imdelltra, which I have high hopes for as a medication that has the potential to bring Amgen to one of the leading positions in the small-cell lung cancer therapeutics market, which is expected to grow to $23 billion by 2035.

From a technical analysis perspective, Mr. Market continues to hold Amgen's share price near the VWAP [$286.16], preventing either the "bulls" or the "bears" from gaining an advantage and finally breaking out of the tedious corrective pattern called the "triangle" that runs between $255 and $324.

In my opinion, this fluctuation also reflects institutional investors' reluctance to invest in Amgen or the ETFs it's a part of, including VanEck Biotech ETF [Amgen weight is 14.17%] (BBH), iShares Biotechnology ETF [6.54%] (IBB), and Invesco Nasdaq Biotechnology ETF [6.52%] (IBBQ).

But despite this, there are two more important questions that I ask myself as well.

If Amgen achieves success in R&D? If its portfolio of medications expands?

The short answer is yes.

Repatha is expanding its presence in the cardiovascular drugs market

So, one of the latest key milestones that, in my view, will strengthen Amgen's balance sheet in the short term is that on August 25, the FDA approved Repatha for use by people who have high levels of LDL-C, or as it is called "bad cholesterol," and who also have an raised risk for developing MACE, but who have not yet been diagnosed by a cardiologist with a particular cardiovascular disease.

In general, when I looked at the data provided by the CDC, I was deeply saddened by the current situation, as approximately 25 million adults had total cholesterol levels [calculated as LDL-C+HDL-C+20% of triglycerides] above 240 mg/dL, a level that increases the risk of plaque formation inside blood vessels, which can lead to angina, heart attack, or stroke.

As a result, these data only strengthen my belief that Repatha's commercial prospects are bright, despite intensified competition in the PCSK9 inhibitors market from Novartis' Leqvio (NVS) and Sanofi/Regeneron's Praluent (SNY) (REGN).

Despite competition, demand for it remains high.

To be more precise, sales of Amgen's anti-PCSK9 monoclonal antibody reached $696 million in the three months ended June 30, 2025, up 30.8% year-on-year.

Now it's time to discuss Amgen's financial results for Q2 2025, which, among other things, reinforce my confidence that its stock has a more than attractive risk/reward ratio.

Amgen's revenue has hit its all-time high, thanks in part to Tezspire and Evenity

Its revenue reached $9.18 billion in Q2 2025, beating the consensus estimate by about $261.9 million, as well as my best-case scenario by $80 million.

Overall, it increased by 9.4% year-on-year and 12.6% quarter-on-quarter, which, in my opinion, is a clear signal to Wall Street that declining sales of Xgeva, as well as the weak performance of Enbrel [etanercept], caused in part by its lower efficacy relative to AbbVie's Rinvoq/Skyrizi (ABBV) and Eli Lilly's Taltz (LLY) in treating psoriatic arthritis, are no longer having a noticeable impact on Amgen's total revenue.

On the other hand, to the delight of the "bulls," Amgen's non-GAAP earnings demonstrated stronger growth.

So it was $6.02, which is 22.1% more than in the second quarter of 2024, despite the continued increase in Amgen's R&D expenses [$1.45 billion in Q2 2024 vs. $1.74 billion in Q2 2025].

Why are its R&D costs growing?

In my opinion, this is primarily due to the extremely expensive late-stage clinical programs for Imdelltra, rocatinlimab, and its potential competition to Eli Lilly's Mounjaro/Zepbound and Novo Nordisk's Ozempic/Wegovy (NVO) called MariTide, which I discussed in great detail in the article "Amgen Stock: Why I Still Rate It A 'Buy'".

What caused Amgen's revenue and earnings to grow so sharply?

In the table below, I have noted the sales of not only its key medications, but also most of them, to understand and answer this question more comprehensively.

First, in Amgen's osteoporosis franchise, besides Xgeva, which I mentioned earlier, another medication that is beginning to feel pressure from its biosimilars, such as Samsung Bioepis' Ospomyv/Xbryk, Sandoz Group's Wyost/Jubbonti (OTCQX:SDZNY), and Celltrion's Stoboclo/Osenvelt, is Prolia [denosumab].

However, like any Big Pharma, Amgen was no exception in effectively planning its long-term initiatives to offset the damage from the loss of exclusivity of its key drugs [in this article, these are Prolia and Xgeva].

So, the successor to the denosumab franchise was Evenity [romosozumab], approved by the FDA and EMA for the treatment of osteoporosis in postmenopausal women.

Sales of this anti-sclerostin monoclonal antibody, developed jointly with UCB SA (OTCPK:UCBJF), reached $518 million in Q2 2025, an increase of 32.5% year-on-year and 17.2% quarter-on-quarter, and beating my expectations by $25 million.

Now I'll move on to discussing the oncology franchise that Amgen has high hopes for.

In its portfolio, Blincyto and Imdelltra continue to surprise me with strong performance.

So, sales of its "superstar" called Imdelltra [tarlatamab] reached $134 million in the three months ended June 30, 2025, up more than 10-fold year-on-year and 65.4% quarter-on-quarter, in my opinion, mainly due to the results of a Phase 2 DeLLphi-301 study.

To be more precise, it is because the group of patients taking this CD3/DLL3 bispecific antibody had an ORR [objective response rate] of 40%, a median overall survival of 14.3 months, and a more important endpoint, namely, a median duration of response of 9.7 months [confidence interval: 2.7, 20.7+], that it turned out to be more effective in the treatment of extensive-stage small cell lung cancer than Hycamtin and Jazz Pharmaceuticals' Zepzelca (JAZZ).

In addition, I want to note that Opdivo failed in the CheckMate 331 [NCT02481830] and CheckMate 451 [NCT02538666] clinical trials, which led to Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) withdrawing it for this indication on December 29, 2020.

The same thing happened with Keytruda, which failed to demonstrate efficacy in the Phase 3 KEYNOTE-604 trial, which also led to the withdrawal of its indication in the treatment of people with small cell lung cancer a little later, namely on March 1, 2021.

Let's continue.

Since I have already discussed the results of the Phase 3 DeLLphi-304 study in a previous article, I just want to remind you that Imdelltra significantly increased median overall survival by 5.3 months relative to chemotherapy, thereby strengthening my confidence that it is becoming the "gold standard" in the treatment of this type of lung cancer.

Amgen is currently conducting an extensive clinical development program on Imdelltra, evaluating its efficacy as monotherapy and in combination with AstraZeneca's Imfinzi [durvalumab] (AZN), Asher Bio's etakafusp alfa, and MediLink's YL201 for the treatment of various stages of small cell lung cancer, as well as neuroendocrine prostate cancer.

Risks

It's time to discuss the risks associated with Amgen, which range from weaker sales of Kyprolis [a drug used to treat R/R multiple myeloma] than I previously factored into my DCF model to President Trump's continued promises to lower drug prices.

Takeaway

Amgen isn't exactly Mr. Market's favorite yet.

In my opinion, this is partly logical and fairly understandable, as the AI hype isn't fading, while pressure on the pharmaceutical industry remains due to HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.'s actions, which are controversial, as well as the lack of information about what specific steps Trump and the White House will take to reduce drug prices.

However, this political noise is just that, noise, and will only have a short-term impact on Amgen.

Overall, in addition to the investment theses I discussed earlier in the article, another one is that Amgen raised its 2025 non-GAAP diluted EPS guidance from $20-$21.2 to $20.2-$21.3.

Therefore, I continue to cover Amgen with a Buy rating.

data.title: S&P 500: Buy Put Spreads Before The Tide Turns
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Weidong Li, CFA as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Since the April low that followed the tariff announcement, U.S. equities have surged astonishingly in a remarkably short time. The S&P 500 (SPX) is up about 35%, the tech-heavy Nasdaq is up more than 40%, and the market continues to notch new highs almost every session. Euphoria is rampant. Because prices have far outpaced corporate earnings and economic growth, the market's underlying fundamentals have deteriorated, increasing the odds of a correction. In this backdrop-and with volatility still subdued-adding portfolio insurance through options, particularly well-chosen put spreads, looks like a sensible strategy.

Overbought market

The US equity market is hovering at an elevated level in terms of valuation; there are multiple warning signs out there, but here I would just list three commonly known measures to illustrate my point: the Shiller P/E ratio, the Buffett indicator and S&P dividend yield versus bond yield.

The Shiller P/E (or CAPE) is the market's price divided by the average of real, inflation-adjusted earnings over the past 10 years, used to smooth cycles and gauge valuation. Right now, this ratio stands at 39.4 versus a 20-year average of 27.1.

Secondly, the Buffett indicator, which compares a country's total stock market capitalization to its gross domestic product (market cap/GDP) to assess whether the equity market is over- or undervalued, stands at 218%, far above the 165% threshold for being significantly overvalued.

Third, compare the S&P 500's dividend yield with Treasuries: it sits about 2.4 percentage points below the 2-year and 2.8 points below the 10-year, signaling that equity investors are accepting far less income than risk-free bonds and are thus counting on strong earnings growth and price appreciation to supply the equity risk premium.

When putting these readings alongside historical averages, it appears quite clear to me that market valuation is overstretched. Therefore, I suspect the market's defiant upswing is powered more by investor sentiments than by economic fundamentals.

Sentiment is pushing the market higher

Since the abrupt dip in April right after the tariff shock, the market has pulled up dramatically without improving fundamentals, investing community has become saturated with positive news and rosy prospects, especially in the last couple of months, such as the anticipated Fed rate cuts, hopes for a de-escalation in trade wars, massive corporate buybacks, and an exuberant belief in an AI-driven technology revolution, etc. Together they have provided investors the courage to pay ever-higher multiples for the same stream of earnings. Prices have risen much faster than profit forecasts, suggesting multiple expansion rather than profitability improvement is keeping the party going.

One particular worrying sign that's worth pointing out is the narrowing of market leadership. A handful of mega-cap technology firms now accounts for an outsized share of index composition (see chart below), creating a self-reinforcing loop: passive ETF flows and benchmark indexing investment push more capital into market winners, which further lifts their weights in the index. The S&P 500's representation of the broader market is now heavily skewed towards the high-beta, high-P/E ratio, high-hope tech sector. This concentration could potentially amplify market volatility once the exodus begins; it's simply too crowded for everyone to unwind on time. Every step the market climbs further in this backdrop means the pendulum will strike harder once it loses upward momentum and strikes back down.

Moreover, sentiment and emotions are often sticky and contagious; they tend to be widespread and lingering for quite some time. In that process, investors' fear of missing out, reckless momentum chasing, and the false belief that "this time is different" keep market participants pouring into narrower and narrower trades. Such rallies can persist, but they are inherently fragile: when every positive expectation is already priced in, any disappointment can cause the flip quickly.

Market cyclicality

In the world of investment, few things are certain; shocks are common occurrence, and surprises are everywhere. However, the market's cyclicality is not one of them; it's quite certain that prices and valuations do not move in a straight line but oscillate through expansions and contractions. These cycles are caused by a combination of objective drivers and subjective forces. Business cycles induce fluctuations in company earnings, interest rates, liquidity, and credit quality, all of which translate into the market's waxing and waning. On the subjective side, investor sentiment, risk appetite, and behavioral biases create feedback loops that push prices away from fundamentals and then back again. The result is a recurring zig-zag path of market movement, with highly variable length and amplitude across episodes.

"History doesn't repeat itself, but it often rhymes". While no two cycles are identical, the duration and magnitude of each cycle could be very different, but the pattern of mean reversion in profit margins and valuation multiples is persistent; the reoccurrence of market ups and downs is near certain. For investors, the message is unmistakable: assuming that today's favorable conditions will persist indefinitely is a dangerous illusion. If the objective is long-term value preservation, then building portfolio insurance is not optional-it is essential.

Buying SPY put spreads as a portfolio hedge

No one could predict the exact course of market movement, yet we could all prepare for the future, and options are powerful tools for hedging portfolio positions. Particularly when volatility is subdued and premiums are relatively inexpensive, carefully constructed option strategies can provide effective downside protection in a cost-efficient manner. Based on the market analysis I have put forward, I advise the purchase of put option spreads in SPY to hedge against market correction.

I will use one example to illustrate my point. The following screenshot is the option chain of SPY in the Dec '25 expiry, taken on 16 Sep at an SPY spot price of 660.2:

A bull spread constructed with puts involves purchasing a higher strike put and simultaneously selling a lower strike put. For instance, one could buy a SPY December 2025 650 put and sell a SPY December 2025 640 put, thereby creating a long position in the SPY Dec '25 650-640 put spread. Based on current pricing, one contract of this spread costs approximately $237. The simplified payoff profile is as follows:

Maximum loss at expiry

Maximum gain at expiry

Breakeven point

Now, consider a market portfolio valued at $100,000. Allocating just 1% of the portfolio to this strategy would allow the purchase of four contracts of the Dec '25 650-640 put spread. The effect on portfolio outcomes under different market scenarios is shown below:

This example demonstrates how put spreads can smooth portfolio payoffs by mitigating downside losses. The strategy modestly reduces upside participation but provides meaningful protection in the event of a market correction, thereby improving the overall risk-return profile of the portfolio.

Moreover, a bull put spread is often preferable to outright put purchases when the anticipated market correction is expected to be limited. It's more cost-efficient and thus less sensitive to time decay.

A bull put spread strategy is also preferable to using VIX-tracking ETFs as a portfolio hedge, since movements in VIX ETFs do not exhibit a reliable one-to-one correlation with SPY and are further disadvantaged by negative roll yield when the VIX future term structure is in contango.

Risks of bull put spread strategy

Timing, position management, and mental resilience are crucial to this strategy's success. Getting the timing right is inherently difficult: overvaluation doesn't mean prices will fall tomorrow, and markets can stay expensive-or climb further-for quite some time. A practical approach is to anchor trades to the economic calendar: identify likely catalysts and establish put spreads around them-Federal Reserve rate decisions, monthly inflation releases, and mega-cap tech earnings, for example:
