Disciplined position management is essential. Size your put spreads so they're large enough to provide meaningful protection in a correction, yet small enough not to dent annual returns. As a rule of thumb, I would allocate about 1-2% of portfolio value to downside hedges.

Another material risk is psychological: withstanding the stress of being out of step with the herd. Maintain conviction by anchoring decisions to a written, evidence-based thesis and a predefined trading plan. Discipline is the best defense against emotional fluctuation.

Bottom Line

U.S. equities look rich and sentiment-driven, and market cycles make a drawdown likely even if timing is uncertain. A small, disciplined allocation (1-2% of portfolio value) to SPY put spreads while volatility is subdued can provide cost-effective, defined-risk protection against a typical 5-15% pullback and avoids the decay issues of VIX ETFs. Time entries around known catalysts, and follow a written plan to manage positions and emotions.

data.title: Data Center Development Opportunities In Secondary Markets
data.description:
By Casey Miller, Managing Director, Portfolio Management | Paul Lewis, Director - European Data Centres | Sebastian Dooley, CFA, Senior Fund Manager | Matt Hackman, Portfolio Manager

Demand for data centers is booming. In primary markets, available capacity is minimal, driving vacancy rates to historic lows, less than 2% in most markets. At the same time, new development has been increasingly constrained. Lead times for critical equipment such as transformers, switches and generators and long delays for permitting and power interconnection have doubled timelines in many cases.1

Now, with exponentially more demand from the AI revolution, power and land itself is in short supply in many primary markets. Suitable sites with a clear path to permitting and access to utility power are increasingly scarce (as Exhibit 1 demonstrates). In some, Dublin and Amsterdam, for example, regulators have imposed moratoriums on new data center builds due to a lack of power infrastructure.

Availability of suitable sites with a clear path to permitting and access to utility power

As we noted last year, the majority of data center capacity remains concentrated in primary markets.2 This is true in both the U.S. and Europe, though in Europe the share of capacity concentrated in primary markets has been declining since Q1 2021. We continue to invest in primary markets when appropriate sites are available for development or existing facilities become available. However, to meet the growing demand for capacity expected over the next 3-5 years, we must also explore development opportunities beyond the primary markets.

The factors we look for in a market

The fundamental factors determining data center market attractiveness have remained largely consistent throughout time. However, a market’s performance against these fundamentals may evolve over time, along with the comparative significance of individual factors. (For example, as data centers expand in scale, land and power availability become more important.)

Availability of power and land

The availability of suitable sites with a clear path to power is fundamentally critical. Permitting and access to utility power is indispensable, particularly as data center campuses expand and become more power intensive. This factor has constrained development in some markets before; New York/New Jersey and Silicon Valley are examples where power and land constraints have been an inhibiting factor for many years. Until recently, most primary markets had many suitable sites with a clear path to permitting and access to utility power. Now, power and land are constrained in many primary markets, as Exhibit 1 makes clear, and thus is the first (though not only) consideration factor for data center developers.

Cost of reliable power

Power reliability is table stakes; data center developers will not build where power is not reliable. (Although in highly power-constrained markets, we are seeing some developers willing to rely on other sources of power generation for the short term while waiting for reliable utility power interconnection.) Beyond reliability, cost is an important factor because power is the single largest operating expense in a data center—about 20% of the total operating cost. Even a relatively small difference in cost per kilowatt hour can make a significant difference in the total operating cost of a data center.

Power cost by market

Tax incentives

In Europe, tax incentives to entice data center development are not prevalent. In the U.S., in the early days of the data center boom, many states offered sales tax exemptions or other tax incentives to entice data center development. Many primary markets still do, though some have pulled back, or attempted to. (In Georgia, for example, the legislature suspended the state’s data center sales tax exemption, though the governor vetoed the bill.)

Following the pattern set by primary U.S. markets 10-15 years ago, many secondary U.S. markets are now offering tax incentives to entice data center development. Tax incentives can be a substantial benefit for developers, in many cases reducing data center deployment costs significantly.

In the U.S., even if a market may otherwise be a good candidate for data center development, a lack of tax incentives is a significant deterrent. This is one of the principal reasons Denver is not on our list of secondary markets, though that may change, as the Colorado legislature recently began discussions of implementing a tax incentive.

Low risk of natural disasters

Over time, cloud providers have developed data center availability zones and designed their applications to failover automatically to another data center in the event of downtime. Nevertheless, in leased facilities, most tenants still expect high uptime; therefore, selecting a location with minimal natural disaster risk continues to remain a key consideration.

Data center climate risk factors include but are not limited to flood zones, hurricane paths, wildfire threats, drought patterns, sea-level rise, and extreme temperature trends. Data centers are especially vulnerable to climate risks due to the disruption of operations through power outages, flooding, or cooling system failures. Additionally, rising temperatures increase cooling costs and energy consumption, directly impacting operational expenses and reliability.

Proximity to population centers

Given the speed of the long-haul fiber networks in the U.S. and Western Europe and the edge presence that hyperscalers have in most markets (enabling content caching), latency doesn’t require very close proximity to end users for most use cases. (Exceptions include applications such as financial trading and AI inference, | where milliseconds make all the difference and proximity is critical to minimize latency.)

In most cases, the reason to locate data centers in proximity to population centers is to have access to skilled labor. Constructing the large, hi-tech campuses required by the users of todays modern data center demands many construction workers, many of whom must be skilled. Although populated markets face more intense competition for these workers, they also benefit from a broader talent pool and educational institutions generating new skilled graduates.

Proximity to primary markets

Data centers tend to locate in clusters, in part due to the high cost of setting up a new cloud region or availability zone. As a result, even as development is increasingly occurring outside of primary markets, the top secondary markets are more likely to be in close proximity to primary markets. For example, Reno as a secondary market in proximity to Silicon Valley, the Carolinas in proximity to Atlanta and Northern Virginia, Berlin in proximity to Frankfurt.

The secondary markets that make the cut

Exhibit 6: Upcoming U.S. and European data center markets

A note on development in far-flung locations

There have recently been a number of high-profile announcements of data center developments in markets such as Mississippi and Montana in the U.S. and Greece in Europe. There are markets that have significant power and land available but meet few of the other criteria we’ve described here (e.g., they’re not close to population centers or primary markets and not on long-haul fiber lines). Development of AI training facilities in such far-flung locations is compelling from a power and land perspective. But these markets’ lack of alignment on other factors calls into question the liquidity of a data center there if it couldn’t easily be repurposed for other applications.

Investment considerations

The data center development strategies that worked in the past no longer work. Meeting the demand for capacity that will come online in the next 3-5 years requires seeking development opportunities outside of primary markets. But which secondary markets are the best candidates? Identifying which new markets represent the best investment opportunities requires experience and expertise to know what factors end users care most about and which markets best meet those criteria.

Conclusion

The data center development strategies that worked in the past no longer work. Meeting the demand for capacity that will come online in the next 3-5 years requires seeking development opportunities outside of primary markets. But which secondary markets are the best candidates? Identifying which new markets represent the best investment opportunities requires experience and expertise to know what factors end users care most about and which markets best meet those criteria.

Footnotes

(1) Learn more in our recent paper, How data center developers are powering AI.

(2) Learn more in Most new data center capacity is still in primary markets.

Original Post

data.title: Nvidia's $100B Catalyst Rewrites The Future
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) has always meant accelerated computing, but the recent $100 billion investment in OpenAI shifts its direction from hyperscale cycle-linked dependency to utility-like demand visibility. Blackwell Ultra ramp-up and order growth of sovereign AI with a complementary ordering of RTX PRO servers increasing penetration throughout enterprises were already making Nvidia the foundation of AI infrastructures.

The OpenAI deal provides the missing ingredient, though as compact scale at an order of magnitude no rival has. The transaction doesn't simply validate Nvidia’s software and hardware stack, it drives downstream adoption from developers worldwide through sovereigns and enterprises. Nvidia has evolved from riding quarterly demand cycles to becoming structurally entrenched as the backbone of the long-term intelligence economy.

Where the earlier focus was on Nvidia proving its full-stack model, the new reality is defined by structural demand visibility, with the $100 billion OpenAI deal recasting its growth trajectory from hyperscaler cycles to entrenched AI infrastructure.

OpenAI $100B Deal: Numbers Behind the Transformation

OpenAI deal is one of the largest tech infra demand commitments of all time. For Nvidia, it secures multiyear demand clarity at a size that may redefine annual number forecasts. Current consensus forecasts $206 billion revenue in FY26 and $274 billion in FY27. If OpenAI’s $100 billion commitment secures incremental orders over the next two years, it may grow revenue through 5–10% per annum, sending FY26 into $220–230 billion and beyond $300 billion in FY27.

The software implications are equally substantial. OpenAI’s recently revised open-weight models, oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b, are individually optimized for Nvidia’s GB200 NVL72 systems. Inference tests show throughput of 1.5 million tokens per second, a tribute to Nvidia’s cost and performance leadership. As the models gain worldwide traction, nations and companies will use Nvidia-compatible infrastructure and optimize efficiency worldwide, making CUDA and DGX Cloud the de facto standard.

This creates a robust flywheel. The more Nvidia-optimized AI models, the greater the developer base of its stack, pushing more GPU demand as well as system and network demand. With OpenAI setting the standard, the ecosystem multiplying effect spreads through enterprises, startups, and sovereigns.

There are risks, mainly regulatory issues with compute concentration and hyperscaler pushback. But the size of the deal and ecosystem alignment make it next to impossible for competitors to match in the near term. Of more substance, it transforms Nvidia from one of the suppliers of accelerators to the de facto infrastructure provider of frontier AI. Success with OpenAI could anchor the inflection point at which Nvidia’s growth transitions from hyperscaler-cyclical growth to structural, utility-like visibility within the intelligence economy.

Why Networking, Software, and Sovereign AI Tilt the Future

Even while the OpenAI pact fosters a new benchmark of demand vision, Nvidia's longer-term trajectory appears equally impressive gauged both against marketplace imperatives and leading rival Advanced Micro Devices (AMD).

Nvidia Blackwell architecture is speeding up more rapidly than any prior generation. Production has already reached about 1,000 racks per week and will further gain speed during Q3 as announced by the CEO. Blackwell Ultra, the next-generation Blackwell architecture, contributed tens of billions of revenue during its first completed quarter, a launch record of a semiconductor. It embodies Nvidia’s platform’s unprecedented integration spanning GPUs, networking, and systems.

AMD, on the other hand, is gaining traction with MI350 accelerators, namely with cost-savvy deployments and with customers looking at a second-source solution as a hedging strategy against a risk of concentration. Compute efficiency and cost competitiveness are AMD's strengths, but differentiation of Nvidia no longer lies with sheer performance. Networking revenue surged a spectacular 98% year-on-year to $7.3 billion and demonstrated that NVLink fabrics, InfiniBand, and Spectrum-X Ethernet are crucial ingredients in scaling out AI clusters. AMD doesn't have this end-to-end portfolio of networking and therefore has no option but to rely upon third-party interconnects that hardly compete with Nvidia's stack-tightly-integrated offering.

Another strategic divergence is software. Accelerated computing still has no alternative to CUDA as a programming model. With each model optimized for Nvidia silicon, as we see with OpenAI’s oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b, switch costs increase. ROCm from AMD is coming of age, yet ecosystem inertia pushes towards Nvidia, especially as enterprises and sovereigns implement off-the-shelf Nvidia solutions optimized for CUDA.

Even the business itself is changing. The narrative has shifted from training AI towards one of inference with cost efficiency and scale at top of mind. Nvidia’s GB200 NVL72 systems process 1.5 million tokens per second and are setting a standard of inference economics. This places the business well to dominate the trillion dollar end of the decade forecast of the inference marketplace with AMD still largely occupied with training workloads.

Sovereign AI programs further widen Nvidia’s moat. Over 3,000 exaflops of Blackwell-based systems are being built out of Europe, and French, German, and British initiatives are setting up domestic AI factories. These deployments are more than a question of performance; they bring Nvidia into the digital sovereignty initiatives of nation-states and therefore make Nvidia’s standing both strategic and commercial. With competitive technologies aside, AMD seems much less central to these initiatives, further highlighting Nvidia’s brand and trust lead at national level.

Nvidia is also going beyond hyperscale into industrial applications and robotics as well as enterprises. Its RTX PRO servers are invading mainstream IT infrastructures with performance figures indicating 45x superior performance relative to CPU infrastructures. Up to nearly 90 global companies worldwide, like Disney and Hitachi, already deploy the servers with simulation and AI workloads. Concurrently, the Jetson Thor platform with over 2,000 teraflops of compute has enabled robotics firms like Boston Dynamics and Agility Robotics to perform generative reasoning in real time. These are early-stage markets compared to the cloud but further Nvidia runway into physical AI and industrial digitalization.

The long-run direction of Nvidia has clear implications. While AMD presents a legitimate alternative, especially among customers price- or regulation-sensitive, Nvidia’s moat rests upon full-stack integration, sovereign adoption, and software entrenchment. The result is a company with demand vision beyond hyperscalers and into enterprises, governments, and entire industries, bestowing its direction less of a cycle and more of a system than at any point prior.

Why NVDA’s Premium Is Justifiable

First off, Nvidia's price appears stretched. Stock trading at 39.7x FY26 EPS of $4.50 and 28.1x FY27 EPS of $6.34 and price-sales ratios of 21.0x and 15.8x next year and the subsequent year correspondingly are at heights above sector medians of 3–4x revenue and 25–30x earnings.

However. valuation must be put in context relative to growth and profitability. With mid-70s non-GAAP gross margins and free cash flow of well over $13 billion per quarter, Nvidia has software-like margins at an infrastructure scale. If OpenAI adds only 5–10% above consensus estimates, EPS can grow towards $7.50–8.00 in FY27, driving the forward P/E into the low-20s, a more usual order of magnitude of durable platform leaders. On an adjusted basis, Nvidia's multiples become less a speculative premium and more a recognition of being at the core of AI infrastructure.

This embodies the valuation dynamic, at consensus, Nvidia looks expensive, but at OpenAI-adjusted growth rates, multiples compress into figures long-term investors have paid for platform monopolies. In other words, if NVDA earns $7.75 and the market values it at 35x earnings (roughly in line with its forward multiple adjusted for growth), the stock would be priced at about $271, an upside of 50% from current levels.

Can Nvidia’s Moat Outrun China, Hyperscalers, and Regulators?

Despite a leading status, Nvidia has several structural risks. Export restrictions to China continue to limit addressable demand and create doubts regarding future architecture. Customer concentration continues as a risk with hyperscalers producing roughly half of data center revenue. If Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG), or Microsoft (MSFT) develop internal chip capabilities at a faster rate, Nvidia faces incremental volume and price pressure.

Competition also looms. AMD’s upcoming MI400 series gains steam as hyperscalers contemplate custom silicon reducing long-term Nvidia exposure. Regulation could get fiercer as governments grow more interested in a single company controlling compute power, particularly with a $100 billion OpenAI investment already underway. And lastly, cycle risks endure: AI uptake at the corporate level may not scale linearly and surprise changes in spending direction could generate volatility.

Nevertheless, such risks are offset by Nvidia’s established ecosystem, unparalleled software stack, and growing product portfolio.

Bottom Line

Nvidia's Q2 FY26 reaffirmed dominance, but the OpenAI $100 billion deal changes one's perspective. By converting cyclical hyperscale demand into contracted clarity, Nvidia is emerging as the ultimate provider of infra of the ultimate intelligence economy.

At consensus, NVDA looks expensive, trading well above sector medians. But if EPS reaches ~$7.75 by FY27 and the market sustains even a 35x multiple, the stock implies ~$271/share. The OpenAI deal increases the odds of such earnings power, meaning today’s premium may compress into a justifiable platform valuation rather than a speculative one.

data.title: Adtran: Negative Earnings And Rising Debt Limiting Upside Gains
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote on ADTRAN Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:ADTN) in April of this year when we reiterated our 'Hold' rating on the US-based network and communications outfit. ADTN has returned close to 23% for qualifying shareholders over the past 5 months or so, which is an excellent return and right in line with the S&P's return over the same timeframe. Encouraging trends were certainly evident in the company's most recent quarterly numbers (Q2 earnings results were released on the 5th of August this year), but we are standing pat on our 'Hold' rating in Adtran for the following reasons.

If we first go to Adtran's long-term technical chart, we see that there has been no change to the stock's sustained pattern of lower highs & lower lows since early 2011. Although ADTN could potentially rally up to the upper multi-year trendline ($19 to $20 a share in due time), the stock would need to print a successful breakout to end the multi-year bear market in earnest.

Intermediate 5-Year Chart

Then, if we turn to a 5-year chart, we see that shares looked like they were about to break out above intermediate resistance recently but were stopped the week of September 15th, 2025, at approximately the $10.60 level for the following reason. The company earlier had announced a convertible 5-year private note offering for $150 million, but then increased the amount to $175 million. Proceeds are intended to go towards rolling over existing debt, while management also intends to enter into capped call transactions, which are instruments that can be used to minimize share dilution over time. Investors did not take to the offering, which is why (considering prevalent overhead technical resistance), we do not foresee any significant upside in Adtran for the time being.

Q2 Trends Demonstrated Robust Customer Acquisition Trends

Management was quick to point to the top-line growth rates on the second quarter earnings call, with strong double-digit growth, particularly in the 'Optical Networking' and 'Access and Aggregation' segments. The 'Subscriber Solutions' segment grew by a smaller 4% compared to Q1, although healthy trends were visible when it came to customer acquisition. In fact, with respect to the Optical Networking category, for example, the clip at which Adtran continues to acquire customers is impressive to say the least, with a further 18 coming on to the company's books in the second quarter. Customer acquisition trends within fiber access in the Access & Aggregation category are also encouraging, with demand for Adtran's products and services continuing to go from strength to strength, as we learned below from the CEO on the Q2 earnings call.

The ongoing success in our Access and Aggregation Solutions is being driven by the technical leadership shown in our SDX portfolio and the corresponding Mosaic Cloud software. In the last 2 years, more than 10 million homes have been passed with fiber using the SDX 6330 alone, highlighting the momentum of the flagship platform in our fiber access portfolio. Demonstrating our ongoing commitment to innovation, we recently connected the first commercial 50 Gig PON customers in the U.K. using our new SDX 6400 series.

Negative Earnings Put Pressure On Sustained Cash-Flow Generation

However, taking into account the ramifications of the Senior offering mentioned earlier, investors need to decipher when the company's top-line growth can essentially 'drop' to the bottom-line. We state this because top-line sequential growth did not transfer over to improved earnings. In fact, operating profit dropped to -$13.5 million (-$3.9 million in Q1), with SG&A rising exactly $10 million sequentially compared to the first quarter.

Suffice it to say, when we see trends like this, it is crucial to ensure the company is not merely 'buying growth' over the near-term (becoming a bigger outfit as a result) but may not be focused on bottom-line profitability to the same degree. The market (investors) will give Adtran some leeway when it comes to the company's negative earnings trends, but it will need to see a clear beeline to positive profitability, especially with near-term EPS revision trends not all that convincing.

Why are the above remarks noteworthy? Well, with Adtran continuing to report negative EBIT numbers, it puts the company's free cash-flow generation efforts under substantial pressure. The cash-flow statement always starts with a company's GAAP profit. This means that if earnings remain negative for a sustained period, management must effectively manage working capital trends to continue generating high levels of cash flow over time.

For example, in Q2 alone, the company generated over $32 million in operating cash flow, which will continue to be needed to pay down debt and allocate capital. The CFO pointed to the sale of non-core assets (such as the Huntsville campus) to raise cash over the near-term, but the company's balance sheet is pretty leveraged already, with over $190 million of long-term debt and over $370 million of intangible assets.

Margins Not Gaining Traction Fast Enough

In the company's most recent second quarter, gross margin came in at 37.31% and operating margin came in at -5.09%. Therefore, irrespective of the revenue gains in the quarter, the above numbers remain more or less on par with trailing 12-month figures, which does little to foster confidence for sustained bottom-line gains.

Remember, the chief instigator of share-price appreciation is bottom-line gains. Therefore, with annual interest expense from the company's debt continuing to hover around the $20 million mark, you feel that management has to develop some type of economies of scale, which means rising revenues must take place alongside rising margins, all things remaining equal.

Conclusion

To sum up, we are reiterating our 'Hold' rating in ADTN due to significant overhead resistance on the technical chart, negative earnings, and rising debt. We acknowledge that revenues in certain segments continue to grow their top lines aggressively, but investors need to see a clear path to bottom-line profitability for prolonged gains to materialize. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Workday: Positive Free Cash Flow Growth Ahead
data.description:
Summary

In my previous coverage on Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY), I recommended a buy rating due to my expectation that WDAY AI's monetization strategy is gaining traction and that its growth outlook is well supported by a solid partnership strategy and the new WorkdayGo launch. This post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock.

The latest Analyst Day provided solid proof points that WDAY’s AI products, partner ecosystem, and new WorkdayGo launch are all scaling faster than expected. Taken together, these drivers suggest that WDAY is not only defending its core HCM business but is also unlocking entirely new growth vectors that should drive long-term FCF/share growth.

AI Monetization

In my previous write-up, I highlighted that AI monetization was starting to gain solid traction, and the disclosures at Analyst Day confirm that this thesis point has not only materialized but has surpassed my expectations. Management finally put hard numbers behind the AI narrative, revealing that WDAY’s portfolio of AI-powered products now generates >$450 million in annual recurring revenue (>5% of FY25 revenue), growing >50% y/y as of 2Q26. Clearly, this is already a sizable business that customers are actively paying for. Even better, management also broke out the newer “agentic AI” SKUs, like the Recruiting and Talent Mobility Agents, which have grown from <$50 million to >$150 million in ARR in just five quarters, representing growth of >200%. Management also quantified the direct top-line impact, stating these new agents are already contributing ~1.5 percentage points of ARR growth to the entire business. I don’t know about you, but this, to me, is the most concrete evidence yet that AI is accelerating growth across the platform.

With that context, I believe management made the right decision to double down on this momentum through acquisitions. I prefer buy vs. build here because time is of the essence, especially given how fast AI innovations are today. It's better for WDAY to acquire and get right into the game (i.e., have products quickly integrated and made available for clients) than to spend R&D dollars and time to build something that may not be as sophisticated. The acquisitions mentioned, Paradox (for ~$1 billion) and Sana (for ~$1.1 billion), are particularly noteworthy. Paradox is a conversational AI platform built for frontline worker recruiting, which represents a massive, underpenetrated market segment (makes up 70% of the global workforce). This acquisition is strategically important because it gives WDAY a specialized solution to target industries with large numbers of these workers, such as retail, healthcare, and hospitality. Through this, it also provides WDAY a valuable "landing point" to sell into new accounts, even those that are not currently using its core HCM products.

Sana, on the other hand, offers an AI-powered enterprise search and knowledge platform. Basically, Sana acts as a central knowledge hub, allowing employees to search for information and execute workflows across various platforms, not just WDAY’s. This acquisition is important for two reasons. First, it dramatically increases user engagement and platform stickiness, as Sana is a highly engaging front end, with users logging in an average of seven times per day. Secondly, it elevates WDAY’s position from a back-office system of record to the primary user interface for knowledge workers (again increasing stickiness and opening up doors to more “expansion”), which management intends to bring to all its 75 million users.

Another catalyst I foresee will accelerate WDAY’s AI adoption is the “Workday Flex Credits.” This consumption-based pricing model allows customers to allocate spending across AI and platform services. The apparent impact is this eliminates friction and ensures customers can scale usage without renegotiations. Once customers see the benefits, they will likely pay for these AI solutions, which basically help in monetization.

Growth levers

The partnership flywheel I expected is now clearly playing out very nicely, and there are new metrics that give more confidence. At the Analyst Day, management mentioned that partners continue to contribute >20% of new customer ACV (marking the 2nd consecutive quarter of >20% contribution). While two quarters don’t really form a trend, this is a very encouraging sign that the new go-to-market [GTM] model is resulting in a scalable growth channel. As I said before, as WDAY scales this partnership ecosystem up, growth should accelerate. I reiterate my view on this. The ecosystem itself has grown dramatically, doubling y/y to over 1,300 partners, including a fivefold increase in sales-focused partners. We should start to see the benefits of this expansion in due time, as this massive expansion extends WDAY’s reach into geographies and customer segments where a direct-only approach would have been too costly or too slow to scale.

The “WorkdayGo” offering, which is aimed at medium enterprises, has also seen solid adoption. As a recap, this is a pre-configured deployment that can be live in under 60 days (reduces adoption friction for SMEs). We now have tangible data to gain a better sense of how solid underlying adoption is—medium enterprise now accounts for more than 50% of new customer ACV. This very clearly signals that WDAY's decision to move downmarket and launch a competitive product is resonating deeply. Also, like I have said before, the addressable demand pool is massive, so this data point is really helpful in framing WDAY’s long-term growth potential.

At the same time, WDAY is also trying to penetrate the US federal government, which is a >$2 billion human capital management opportunity, with the launch of WDAY Government Cloud. This WGC initiative is important and should help WDAY win deals because it provides a standardized, scalable, and secure platform tailored to federal requirements (i.e., federal-specific requirements are directly integrated into WDAY), vs. costly and complex one-off custom projects. The key distinction is that productization makes federal requirements a standard part of the platform, while customization treats them as a unique, client-specific project. Put simply, WDAY’s strategy of "Productization—NOT Customization" should be more scalable and reliable and likely comes with a lower total cost of ownership for its government clients. The launch already shows signs of traction, with management noting strong engagement across the Department of Defense, the Intelligence Community, and civilian agencies. Solid traction here also opens doors for WDAY to penetrate other parts of the government budget (like state, local, and even international public-sector clients).

Taken together, the combination of a scaled partner ecosystem, strong mid-market adoption, and entry into federal paints a very solid growth outlook for WDAY.

Valuation/Guidance

Perhaps the most important development for the long-term investment case is the new financial framework. While bears are likely to focus on the softer subscription revenue CAGR target of 13-14% through FY28 (down from the previous ~15% target for FY27), I believe this is not the right metric to focus on. The important metric is the guide to deliver >20% Free Cash Flow [FCF] per share CAGR through FY28. This FCF/share guide seems achievable even with the 13-14% subscription revenue CAGR, as margins are expanding—adj. EBIT margin is guided for ~35% by FY28, a sharp increase from the previous 30% target for FY27. Also, management is buying back shares—they announced an accelerated $5 billion share repurchase authorization through FY27. This is a significant capital return program that will provide a meaningful tailwind to FCF per share growth.

With that, we can model out WDAY on an FCF basis. Management guided for FY26 FCF of ~$2.65 billion, or 21% y/y, and assuming that continues through FY28, I expect WDAY to generate $3.8 billion in FCF. For a business that is growing FCF at 20%/year, I don’t think the current 22x forward P/FCF multiple is expensive (implied ~4.5% FCF yield, which is just slightly above the US 10-year yield of 4.1%). I could argue that WDAY deserves to trade at a lower yield (i.e., a high multiple) to justify the growth, but even at 22x, I see a 30% upside.

Risk

Management lowered its subscription revenue target to a 13%-14% CAGR through FY28, and should growth continue to decelerate, it could mark a maturing large enterprise HCM market that is reaching saturation. This may shape up the bearish narrative that WDAY long-term FCF/share growth will decelerate meaningfully as well. While WDAY leans on AI to reaccelerate growth, execution is key, in that WDAY needs to make sure customers' concerns over data protection are well met. Any failure to do so here could trigger an exodus on WDAY offerings.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for WDAY remains a buy. The accelerating AI monetization, scaled partner flywheel, adoption in the medium enterprise market, and a push into federal create a compelling long-term growth story. Importantly, I believe WDAY can achieve the new guidance of >20% FCF/share CAGR through FY28 with support from a $5 billion buyback program. At 22x forward P/FCF, I see the current valuation as attractive relative to the growth outlook.

data.title: SharpLink: A Crypto Treasury Play Undermined By Weak Core Operations
data.description:
Opinions are divided when it comes to companies using crypto treasury strategies. On one side, there is enthusiasm, with some investors projecting returns of over 30x on their investment. Others think this is another bubble and that, in the case of another crypto downturn, these companies may suffer even more than the crypto assets they hold. I tend to be optimistic about crypto, more about Bitcoin than the others, but at the moment, I am not bullish on cryptos in general. When it comes to treasury strategies, for some companies like SharpLink (NASDAQ:SBET), I am also not convinced.

The treasury of a company is usually a store of cash to sustain its core business operations or for future strategic acquisitions, but in the end, the main goal of the treasury is to help the main business. If a company is not performing well, at one point, it will run out of funds, having to seek outside investment, through debt or equity, and both options could have a negative impact on investors.

In my view, a company holding crypto assets can be a good way to store value and protect from inflation over the long term, but only for profitable companies that don’t need to be seeking funds every so often because operations aren’t sound enough. To look at it from another perspective, if there are two companies, both executing a similar crypto treasury strategy, but only one of them is profitable in its core business. Which one would you invest in?

Well, of course there are different valuations, but the stock market is not that straightforward. Profitable companies usually end up trading at higher valuations than unprofitable ones. Also, more aggressive investors may use this to their advantage, going long on the profitable company while shorting the other one, which would give these investors a hedge in the case of a crash in the sector and may increase the difference in valuation between these two hypothetical companies.

In the SBET case, I don’t like it firstly because the company has never had a profitable year. If you look at the company’s finances, you will get the idea, but for example, SG&A costs in the last quarter were $2.3 million versus a gross profit of around $0.2 million. SG&A is more than 10 times greater than gross profit, and on a consistent basis. To me, that spells risk, no crypto treasury strategy will fix that, and in the future, the company may continue to lose money, requiring more equity financing, further diluting shareholders.

Another factor is the way the company is structuring its Ethereum holdings. As of today, it holds no debt. All Ethereum bought by SBET was from equity issuance, which diluted shareholders, and in the end, doesn’t create the asymmetrical gains that a company leveraged with debt can have during a crypto bull run, which most investors expect from a treasury strategy. For this strategy to work without debt, the company needs the stock to trade at a premium mNAV to issue more stocks, which is not the case now, according to the company's live dashboard the current mNAV is 0.98x, and there is no guarantee that it will trade at a premium in the future, given that other companies are pursuing the same strategy with Ethereum, like Bitmine Immersion (BMNR).

SharpLink's Business

Outside of the crypto strategy, SharpLink Gaming is an online marketing company that uses fan engagement tools, like quizzes and interactive widgets, to attract more paying customers to online sports betting sites and online casinos. They act as intermediaries and receive performance-based compensation when they successfully refer new customers, but also receive a commission when this customer spends money on games and bets. So, their main goal is to attract new paying customers to online casinos and sports betting sites.

The company has a history of losses despite being in the growing online gambling industry. That is mainly because of a significant SG&A expense, which, in my view, discourages the investment in the company.

In 2019, SG&A was $0.7 million, representing 29% of revenue, but in 2022, SG&A expense skyrocketed to $10.8 million, three times bigger than revenue, which was $3.5 million in 2022.

This sharp increase can be explained by changes in consumer privacy policy that happened in 2021, where Apple, other companies, and government agencies have implemented measures to stop unauthorized sharing of personal data online. Now, when accessing websites, users are explicitly asked whether they consent to cookies and other trackers collecting their data. This change has made it more challenging for marketing companies to target specific audiences, which, in turn, has driven up the cost of online marketing.

Another red flag of the business for me is that salaries and benefits have been consistently greater than the gross profit. This, combined with other expenses, has drained the company’s capital. At the end of 2023, its cash reserves were $2.4 million, and by 2024, it fell to $1.4 million, the business was running out of capital, and they were looking for options to keep operations going.

What could turn out to be positive is that after the acquisition of a 10% stake in a crypto casino. Now that the company’s valuation is tied to how much Ethereum it holds, this could help boost SBET’s valuation, although SBET has yet to generate meaningful revenue from this investment.

Risk

The biggest risk of the thesis is Ethereum prices, which also follow Bitcoin prices to some extent. Recently, I wrote an analysis on the Bitcoin cycle around the halving and the correlation with lower interest rates, explaining that the period of falling rates was actually detrimental to Bitcoin prices due to better opportunities in other less risky markets. I also outlined the lack of correlation with the M2 money supply during important downturns, and more. In the analysis, I explained that I am expecting lower Bitcoin prices starting around November and throughout 2026, but I am still waiting for confirmation. If this turns out to be the case, then Ethereum and the other cryptocurrencies may follow suit. Considering that, in the past, cryptocurrencies have dropped more than 80%, I think it is wise to be on the lookout for this scenario to happen.

Given that SBET is not profitable, and other companies are doing basically the same thing, but with stronger operational finances, like Bitmine Immersion (BMNR), cited earlier, there is a risk of SBET not being traded at a premium to its Ethereum holdings, thus not being able to execute its strategy properly. Yet, with SBET constantly losing money in its operations, the company may have to dilute shareholders even with a lower valuation in comparison with its Ethereum holdings, which would be detrimental to shareholders.

To simplify, the risks are that Ethereum prices may fall. The dependency of the company to be traded at a premium to mNAV in order to execute its strategy, which may not happen. Also, due to its core operations not being profitable, SBET may have to dilute shareholders below mNAV.

Conclusion

Investors are seeking ways to be exposed to crypto in a leveraged manner. However, that may not happen with SBET since there are better options in the market with better financial results from operations. Additionally, my view on cryptocurrency prices for next year is not positive, given the historical pattern around the cycle and better opportunities in other markets, with much less risk, arising from the period of lowering interest rates. For now, the stock is a Sell.

data.title: Brookfield Renewable: Trump's Policy Gambit Vs. Brookfield's Global Diversification
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The grass might seem greener for Brookfield Renewable Partners (NYSE:BEP) investors looking at high-yield stocks with double-digit yields.

I've been there, and I'm here to tell you, it's not.

Many high-yielding stocks operate an ice-cube business model, whose shortcomings inevitably translate to a decline in NAV and share price. In fact, entire sectors, such as Business Development Companies "BDCs" typically have high yields, but also NAV, dividend, and price per share declines. Their mandatory dividend distribution requirements mean that these entities can't build up loan loss reserves. Sometimes it's about leverage, as seen in the case of AGNC Investment Corp (AGNC). Sometimes it's risky assets, such as the case of Eagle Point Credit (ECC) and Oxford Lane (OXLC).

Brookfield Corporation (BN) and its publicly traded subsidiaries, such as BEP, are a different breed. Their mission, asset quality, and regulatory landscape are completely different. They emphasize stability, dividend growth, and long-term capital preservation.

I've done the math. Take Eagle Point Credit, which yields 24%. Between October 2015 and the present, for each 100 trading days, there were fewer than 13 days in which an investor could have bought shares and locked an annualized total return of 8% or higher. The vast majority of investors are sitting on annualized yields of 5% or lower. The same goes for Oxford Lane, AGNC, and many others.

The stability of BEP, and all of the companies under the BN umbrella, isn't something to scoff at. They're built to last through economic cycles, unlike many high-yield funds out there.

BEP now has a yield on cost of 5.78%. Even when ignoring its growth, it's already generating more than most high-yield tickers would generate in total returns, when including the share price declines in the equation.

The Brookfield Stack

BN is huge. It owns interests in oil & gas pipelines, seaports, road toll stations, real estate (including residential, commercial, and hospitality properties), data centers, communication towers, and utility companies (electric, gas, water, and waste). Additionally, it operates hydroelectric, solar, wind, and nuclear power plants.

To make it easier for investors to gain exposure to specific asset classes, BN spins off its subsidiaries into standalone public companies, while retaining a significant interest.

If you're looking to gain exposure to everything, buy BN.

BEP holds renewable energy assets. BN has a 48% ownership in BEP (p. 62).

Investors can gain access to BEP's portfolio either through its ticker (partnership units), or through exchangeable common equity shares (NYSE:BEPC) and (TSX:BEPC:CA), which are exchangeable 1:1 with BEP units.

Looking Past Accounting Noise

Although BEP reports IFRS losses, this is largely due to non-cash items, such as long-term hedging derivatives, which impact short-term performance, and inflated depreciation resulting from IFRS asset revaluation, a unique accounting method that allows companies to report assets at their fair value instead of their historical value. This means that depreciation no longer reflects historical costs, rendering it less useful in assessing performance. Revaluation accounting is particularly an issue for a company such as BEP, whose entire model is based on asset recycling: purchasing power generation assets, stabilizing them, and subsequently selling a portion to lock in capital gains.

H1 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021 2020 2019
Earnings per LP unit -$0.58 -$0.89 -$0.32 -$0.60 -$0.69 -$0.61 -$0.26

For this reason, management emphasizes Funds from Operations "FFO" in its presentations.

Examining the FFO/share chart below, we observe a strong upward trend. However, we also observe that FFO/share declined from 2014 to 2016. This period coincided with a drought in Brazil, which impacted hydroelectric power generation. The company also raised equity for the first time since its spin-off in 2011, adding to the pressure on FFO/share. The bottom line is, while BEP's operations are tied to contracted power supply agreements, often with inflation escalators, it is not immune to weather-related volatility. That's one factor that rating agencies consider when assigning BEP a BBB+ rating, one of the highest in the renewable infrastructure market.

Grid Bottlenecks Become an Opportunity

BEP currently enjoys an exceptionally strong outlook, thanks to surging demand for power, which has become the primary bottleneck in the buildout of AI infrastructure.

BEP has long supplied power to big tech companies, but the size of the commitments has become substantial. For example, Microsoft (MSFT) recently signed a framework for a potential 10.6 GW capacity over the next 5 years. For perspective, BEP's current proportionate capacity sits at 46 GW. Alphabet has also recently signed a 3 GW contract.

We view these developments as accommodative for BEP, allowing it to achieve its long-term goal of growing FFO/share by 10% annually.

Diversification Cushions Policy Shift

Trump's executive orders and the recent budget bill have created some tailwinds for the renewable market, but not as disruptive as many thought. For example, while Trump is phasing down support for wind and solar, current projects and those commencing construction before July 2026 are still eligible for tax credits.

Moreover, the big, beautiful bill naturally impacts assets in the United States. BEP is well diversified, with more than 43% of FFO derived from international markets, including Mexico and Canada, which are included in the North America segment.

Finally, the Trump administration is turning its back mainly on wind and solar, which are now established technologies, with little need for federal support to stand on their own. There is no significant impact on the federal support for hydro power and storage, which, combined, constitute 53% of BEP's FFO.

Baby Bonds Sitting on Contracted Assets

Some analysts have noted BEP's high debt load, which on an IFRS basis is reported as $37.7 billion (p. 2). However, we need to adjust this figure for the minority partners' portion of this debt in the same way we adjust FFO and adjusted EBITDA. All in all, BEP's portion of this debt is roughly $16.6 billion (p. 23). This includes various tranches of corporate and non-recourse debt, with a weighted average interest rate of 5.3%. This roughly translates to an annual interest expense of $886 million.

Note: In Q2 2025, management reported proportionate interest expense at $238 million, which, when annualized, is slightly above our interest expense run rate calculated above. The proportionate weighted average interest rate for non-recourse debt is reported as 5.6%, compared to 4.6% for corporate debt (p. 3). Our 5.3% estimate is calculated based on the interest rate schedule on p. 23 of the Q2 2025 quarterly filing, which includes both corporate and non-recourse debt.

Annualizing H1 2025 Adjusted EBITDA of $1.3 billion, we expect a proportionate interest rate coverage ratio of 3x, a comfortable place to be for (BEPH), (BEPI), and (BEPJ) baby bond holders, carrying a yield-on-cost of 6.9%, 7.0%, and 7.1% annually. The only drawback to these bonds is that they're perpetual, with no maturity date. Thus, they don't offer a clean exit route, exposing investors to the risk of changes in the market position of the underlying operations.

Dividends Payout Ratio Covered

In the six months ended June 2025, the company distributed $0.75 per unit (including BEPC), compared to $1.04 in proportionate FFO, resulting in an adjusted cash payout ratio of approximately 72%. This provides some cushion against fluctuations in FFO. Note: Proportionate FFO is net of interest expense and preferred distribution, offering us confidence in the payout ratio above.

Given that BEPC trades at a premium to BEP, it is worth considering the latter as an alternative.

Note: Investors might need to file additional tax forms related to holding BEP partnership units.

Final Thoughts and Risks

Many high-yield stocks appear attractive to income-oriented investors. However, after accounting for share price declines, most end up with total returns at the same level, or even lower, than those of investment-grade companies.

Despite the recent regulatory changes, we still see BEP as an attractive investment, with ~230 GW of pipeline projects, offering a multi-year growth runway for long-term investors. The momentum created by the buildup of AI data centers is translating into huge commitments from hyperscalers, as reflected in the recently announced deals.

Despite the reported IFRS losses, the interest on BEP's baby bonds is covered by profitable, cash-generating, power supply assets. The diversification across different asset classes and geographies adds to the allure of BEP and its baby bonds.

The Trump administration's detrimental stance towards wind and solar has not yet translated to a monumental change in the investment thesis. The situation remains fluid, but we remain bullish on all four BEPs: BEP, BEPH, BEPI, and BEPJ.

The primary risk is tied to power rates, economic and environmental cycles, which could have a significant impact on FFO, despite the contracted power supply agreements.

Overall, we see a positive risk/reward balance in this ticker.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
